prompt:
Emma's Story

Part 1

It's Sunday and you're at brunch with friends Sara and Jake, catching up. All of you were at the same party last night, along with mutual friends Kyle and Emma (who is 19 years old). Sara starts talking about what happened at the party after you left to go home early.

    Sara: You won't believe what happened last night after you left. Emma got totally wasted. That 19-year-old does not know how to hold her liquor! She ended up in some weird situation with Kyle.
      You: What do you mean?
    Sara: Well, Emma was very drunk. At some point, she and Kyle ended up alone in the back room together, and well, you know how these things go.
      You: So, what happened exactly?
    Sara: I don't know all the details. Kyle's words were “we might have blacked out and had sex.” You can't blame Kyle though; guys like him get frisky when they're drunk. Plus, Emma didn't exactly say 'no' and definitely seemed into him earlier.
    Jake: I doubt Emma will remember what happened. I'm sure Kyle wasn't trying to hurt her.
Your stomach drops. You told Emma you were leaving the party early and she promised that she would be ok. Plus, it's unsettling to hear Sara and Jake minimize what could be a serious situation with such a casual tone.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still shaken after learning that Kyle likely took advantage of Emma while intoxicated. A few days have passed since the incident, and you know that evidence collection from a sexual assault examination is time sensitive. Concerned, you decide to visit Emma at home.
    You: Hey, Emma. What happened after the party the other night? I feel bad for leaving early.
    Emma: I... I don't remember and I don't want to talk about it. I…I just should have left with you.
Your concern for Emma deepens as her eyes well up with tears and her voice trembles with shame.
question: Read part 2 of the scenario. Imagine you decide the best course of action is to say something directly to Emma. What do you say?
students_answer: I heard you and Kyle slept together last night, how are you feeling about that? I know you were pretty drunk, was it consenual?
categories:
| Check In with the Person Targeted | Privately reach out afterward to offer empathy, validate their feelings, and make sure they're okay. | “I saw what happened—are you okay?”, “That situation didn't seem right. How are you feeling about it?”, “You didn't deserve to be treated that way.” |
| Delegate | Get help or involve someone else who can intervene safely or effectively | Ask a peer, supervisor, or friend to join you in speaking up., Contact a leader, bystander intervention advocate, or security for help., Text or alert someone who has more authority to address it. |
| Distract | Interrupt or redirect attention to defuse or change the situation | Do something to remove the victim from the situation., Drop something, spill a drink, or talk loudly on the phone to interrupt the moment., Ask them to help with something (“Can you help me grab something from my car?”). |
| Document | Record or report the incident safely and appropriately | Take notes or video (if safe and legal) to support the person harmed., Write down what you witnessed and when it happened., Share documentation with the person affected or appropriate authorities—not on social media. |
| Encourage Resources or Next Steps | Offer help connecting with support systems, without pressure. | “If you want, I can help you reach out to someone you trust.”, “There are confidential resources if you want to talk through what happened.”, “Would you like me to come with you to report or get support?” |
| Escalate conflict | Be aggressive by saying or doing something that makes the situation worse or more tense. | Publicly shame the person in front of everyone., Yell curse, or scream at the person causing harm., Say you expected better behavior from them. |
| Excusing perpetrator behavior | Defends or rationalizes the behavior of the person causing harm, often framing it as unintentional or acceptable under the circumstances. | Ask the person causing harm if they're “just joking.”, Say the victim misinterpreted what happened., Tell them understand that they probably didn't mean to cause harm. |
| Express Disapproval or Disagreement  | Voice that you disapprove of or feel uncomfortable with the behavior—calm, firm, and professional. | “I don't agree with that kind of talk.”, “That joke made me uncomfortable.”, “This isn't an appropriate place for that conversation.” |
| Name or Call Out the Behavior  | Clearly identify the inappropriate act, language, or conduct to stop it immediately. | “That's not funny.”, “That crosses a line.”, “Let's stop—this isn't OK.”, “Those jokes are making people uncomfortable.” |
| Offer Emotional Support and Validation | Affirm that the person's feelings are real and that they aren't to blame for what happened. | “No one deserves to be treated like that.”, “Your feelings about this are completely valid.”, “It's understandable to feel angry, hurt, or confused right now.” |
| Protect or Support the Person Targeted  | Step in directly to protect, support, or remove someone from risk. | “Hey, are you OK?”, “Give them some space.”, “Let's take a step back.” |
| Question or Challenge the Behavior  | Use questions to prompt reflection or reveal flawed assumptions. | “What makes you think that?”, “How do you know that's true?”, “Do you realize how that might sound to others?” |
| Redirect or Refocus on Professionalism | Steer the conversation or environment back to work standards, values, or appropriate topics. | “Let's keep this conversation work-related.”, “We should focus on the task at hand.”, “Remember our policy about keeping communication professional.” |
| Share a Perspective or Story to Reframe the Issue | Use a personal or relatable story to illustrate the impact or shift perspective. | “I know someone who was hurt by that kind of joke.”, “I've seen how comments like that can really affect people.”, “That reminds me why we need to be careful about what we say.” |
| Victim blaming | Attributes responsibility for the harm to the person experiencing it rather than the person causing it. Implies the victim provoked, consented to, or could have prevented the behavior. | Demand an explanation from the person harmed (“Why didn't you say something sooner?”)., Tell the victim to “toughen up” or “just ignore it.”, Ask why they “let it happen.” |

students_answer: Stopping Emma from drinking could have prevented this whole situation.

prompt:
The Divorcee

Part 1

You work at a military base with your boss, SMSgt Sosa, and co-workers, Amn Davis and Amn Jimenez. SGM Sosa has been going through a tough time—she's recently divorced and clearly searching for connection. Amn Davis is savvy; he noticed SMSgt Sosa's loneliness and knows exactly how to manipulate her need for validation. It started as small, harmless glances and extra attention, but now it feels unprofessional. You and Amn Jimenez are talking in the breakroom when the topic comes up.

    Amn Jimenez: Have you noticed the way Amn Davis and SMSgt Sosa act around each other? They're constantly flirting. At least she seems way more relaxed now; I don't remember her smiling like that!
      You: Oh, I've seen it too. At first, I was happy SMSgt Sosa was finally getting some after her divorce, but now it feels different. Amn Davis is getting away with stuff no one else would.
    Amn Jimenez: Like signing off on his TDY without any of the normal paperwork?! Amn Davis told me, and I quote: “I've got SMSgt Sosa in my pocket.” It's so uncomfortable to watch, and obvious that SMSgt Sosa's loneliness is affecting her decision-making.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

The whole situation is probably none of your business, but after speaking with Amn Jimenez, you can't shake the feeling that SMSgt Sosa is being manipulated by Amn Davis. While his behavior is subtle, his motives are clear.

You feel bad for SMSgt Sosa, who's struggling emotionally, but Amn Davis — who is your friend—is exploiting SMSgt Sosa's vulnerability. You both appreciate that SMSgt Sosa has become distracted and less strict. You wish that Amn Jimenez would say something since she's so concerned about it.

You wonder if the preferential treatment is harmless, especially if there's no clear misconduct. You don't want SMSgt Sosa or Amn Davis to get into trouble, although you feel that what's happening isn't right. Reporting this to your colonel could make things worse for everyone.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: 1. Directly talk to SMSgt Sosa about the situation and your concerns. 2. Confront Davis about his actions.

| Avoidant | Take no action, withdraw, or choose not to intervene in any way. | Pretend to take a phone call so you can leave the area., Reflect on how you might respond sooner next time and prepare wording for future situations. |

| Delegate | Seek assistance from someone else who can intervene appropriately or provide guidance. | Seek advice from HR about the correct course of action., Consult the Sexual Harassment/Assault Response & Prevention (SHARP/SAPR) office for confidential guidance., Ask a mentor or supervisor outside your chain for perspective on how to approach the situation professionally. |
| Direct | Address the behavior or person causing harm directly, calmly, and respectfully. | Confront Amn Davis about his manipulation of SMSgt Sosa., Speak to Amn Davis privately about the potential personal and professional consequences of being involved with a superior., Ask Amn Davis if he understands what he's doing can really hurt SMSgt Sosa. |
| Distract | Interrupt or redirect attention to defuse or change the situation | Ask SMSgt Sosa a neutral work question to redirect her focus (“Ma'am, can you review this form when you have a moment?”)., Invite Amn Davis to help with another task to give Sosa space and break the interaction., Enter the workspace when you notice flirtatious behavior to make your presence known. |

| Hostile Confrontation | Confront in a hostile, threatening, shaming, or emotionally charged way that increases interpersonal conflict. prompt:
Showing Gratitude

Part 1

You work in a warehouse. While organizing inventory, you overhear a conversation between SrA Johnson (your friend and coworker) and 1stLt Franco (your leader). At first, the conversation seems like normal business talk, but soon the tone changes.

      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”

You work in a warehouse. While organizing inventory, you overhear a conversation between SrA Johnson (your friend and coworker) and 1stLt Franco (your leader). At first, the conversation seems like normal business talk, but soon the tone changes.

    SrA Johnson: If I fail this review, then I don't get a promotion, and I'll be reassigned OCONUS.
      1stLt Franco: I hear you, Johnson, but sometimes, it's not just about the work. There are... other ways to get ahead around here. You know what I mean?
    SrA Johnson: No, what do you mean?
      1stLt Franco: Listen, I've got influence around here. I can pull some strings to move you up the ranks, but you'd need to... show me some gratitude. This is just how things are done around here.
    SrA Johnson: It sounds like you're suggesting…
      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.

prompt:
My Roommate's Arrest

Part 1

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

While you've always considered yourself a good friend and someone who stands up for what's right, you're struggling with what to do in this situation. Jordan, your closest friend, has a habit of joking about sensitive topics without realizing the impact, so he probably didn't mean any harm.

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”

question: Active bystanders are motivated to take responsibility in risky situations. What is one positive outcome that could result when a bystander intervenes in situations like this?
students_answer: Removing the threat of sexual assault and supporting the victim.
categories:
| Building Bystander Skills & Confidence  | Taking action because it  builds skills, confidence, or readiness to act in future situations. | Doing something now makes it easier to act the next time., Each time someone intervenes, they become more prepared to handle similar situations in the future. |
| Bystander Avoidance / Diffusion of Responsibility | Avoids personal responsibility for acting, assuming someone else will intervene or that it's not one's place to respond. | “Someone else will step in if it's really a problem.”, “It's not my place to get involved—it's between them.”, “No one else seems concerned, so I don't need to say anything.” |
| Changing Perpetrator Behavior & Attitudes | Taking action because it influences the person causing harm to reflect, reconsider, or change their behavior and attitudes. | The individual may come to understand that their behavior is inappropriate or unacceptable, It might prevent them from doing something they will regret. |
| Cultural or Peer-Norm Justification | Uses social norms, workplace culture, or peer expectations to justify inaction or normalize harmful behavior | “That's just part of the culture here; everyone jokes like that.”, “It's better not to rock the boat—people will think you're uptight.”, “If I speak up, it'll just make things awkward in the group.” |
| Fear of Negative Consequences (Social / Professional) | Avoids intervention out of concern for personal reputation, social rejection, or professional repercussions. | “Saying something could hurt team morale.”, “If I speak up, it'll just make things awkward in the group.”, “Reporting it might make me look like I can't take a joke.” |
| Inspiring Collective Action | Taking action because it motivates or empowers others to step in or reinforces a shared responsibility for safety. | Saying something can inspire others to take action against sexual violence., Speaking up encourages others to do the same when they see harm. |
| Lack of Confidence / Learned Helplessness | Expresses self-doubt or a belief that intervention will be ineffective or make the situation worse | “I wouldn't know what to say anyway.”, “I'd probably just make things worse if I said something.”, “It's not my responsibility to fix other people's behavior.” |
| Minimizing or Dismissing Harm | Downplays the seriousness or impact of the behavior, suggesting it's harmless, normal, or not worth addressing. | “It's just harmless fun; no need to blow it out of proportion.”, “Nobody got hurt, so there's no reason to make it awkward by saying something.”, “People joke like that all the time—it's not a big deal.” |
| Perpetrator-Excusing | Defends or rationalizes the behavior of the person causing harm, often framing it as unintentional or acceptable under the circumstances | “Calling them out might embarrass them—they probably didn't mean any harm.”, “You shouldn't interfere; that's just how some people talk.”, “They didn't intend to offend anyone, so there's no problem.” |
| Preventing Future Harms | Taking action because it helps stop a situation from getting worse or prevents more severe forms of harm (e.g., harassment escalating to assault). | Speaking out against this behavior helps now prevent more severe forms of sexual assault or harassment in the future., It might prevent the situation from escalating. |
| Protecting Teams | Taking action because it strengthens trust, cohesion, and mutual respect within a group, team, or community. | Intervention helps maintain trust and respect within the group, unit, squad or battalion., Trust and respect grow when people take action to protect one another. |
| Recognition and Rewards for the Bystander | Taking action because it results in acknowledgemnt, appreciation or other tangible rewards for the bystander. | Doing the right thing can lead to recognition, commendation, or confidence in your character., Standing up for others earns appreciation and gratitude from your peers. |
| Shaping Norms & Culture Change | Taking action because it challenges harmful norms and helps establish respect, safety, and accountability as community standards. | It stops sexual violence from being seen as normal, acceptable, or “not a big deal.”, It promotes accountability for one's actions and their impact on others. |
| Supporting the Victim | Taking action because the victim feels supported, validated, less isolated, or safer in the moment. | Taking action will help the victim feel validated and supported, The victim will feel supported. |
| Victim-blaming | Attributes responsibility for the harm to the person experiencing it rather than the person causing it. Implies the victim provoked, consented to, or could have prevented the behavior. | “If it really bothered them, they would've said something.”, “If someone flirts a lot, they shouldn't be surprised when others cross the line.” , “They shouldn't have gone to that party if they didn't want that kind of attention.” |

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.

prompt:
Showing Gratitude

Part 1

You work in a warehouse. While organizing inventory, you overhear a conversation between SrA Johnson (your friend and coworker) and 1stLt Franco (your leader). At first, the conversation seems like normal business talk, but soon the tone changes.

    SrA Johnson: If I fail this review, then I don't get a promotion, and I'll be reassigned OCONUS.
      1stLt Franco: I hear you, Johnson, but sometimes, it's not just about the work. There are... other ways to get ahead around here. You know what I mean?
    SrA Johnson: No, what do you mean?
      1stLt Franco: Listen, I've got influence around here. I can pull some strings to move you up the ranks, but you'd need to... show me some gratitude. This is just how things are done around here.
    SrA Johnson: It sounds like you're suggesting…
      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.

prompt:
Showing Gratitude

Part 1

You work in a warehouse. While organizing inventory, you overhear a conversation between SrA Johnson (your friend and coworker) and 1stLt Franco (your leader). At first, the conversation seems like normal business talk, but soon the tone changes.

    SrA Johnson: If I fail this review, then I don't get a promotion, and I'll be reassigned OCONUS.
      1stLt Franco: I hear you, Johnson, but sometimes, it's not just about the work. There are... other ways to get ahead around here. You know what I mean?
    SrA Johnson: No, what do you mean?
      1stLt Franco: Listen, I've got influence around here. I can pull some strings to move you up the ranks, but you'd need to... show me some gratitude. This is just how things are done around here.
    SrA Johnson: It sounds like you're suggesting…
      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.
question: Imagine you decide the best course of action is to say something directly to either SrA Johnson or 1stLt Franco. Who do you speak to and what do you say?
students_answer: I speak to1st lt. Franco and let him know the severity of his comments and actions.

prompt:
My Roommate's Arrest

Part 1

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

While you've always considered yourself a good friend and someone who stands up for what's right, you're struggling with what to do in this situation. Jordan, your closest friend, has a habit of joking about sensitive topics without realizing the impact, so he probably didn't mean any harm.

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”

You are stationed at a military base working with SSgt Quinn, Amn James, and Amn Brown. You notice SSgt Quinn and Amn James step into the hallway for a private conversation. At first, it seems like normal work talk, until you overhear SSgt Quinn's comment.

    SSgt Quinn (to Amn James): I am a sexual being. Some things cannot be controlled.
      Amn James laughs nervously at SSgt Quinn.
The exchange sounds off, but you're on a deadline, so you ask Amn Brown to check on things. Amn Brown steps out into the hall when you hear a struggle, presumably between SSgt Quinn and Amn James. Amn Brown returns to say:
    Amn Brown (to you): I don't know what exactly happened, but SSgt Quinn pinned Amn James against the wall and grabbed his crotch. He pushed her away, and SSgt Quinn walked off like it was nothing!

Just then, Amn James returns to the workspace, visibly shaken.
    Amn James (to Adm Brown): You ok, man?
    Amn Brown (to Amn James and you): I'm fine. I'm taking my lunch break now.
      Amn James leaves the room.
    Amn Brown (to you): Dude, no one's going to believe a guy got assaulted by a woman.
You didn't directly witness what happened and you're not sure what to do. You want to help, but the situation seems resolved after both SSgt Quinn and Amn Brown left the workplace.

Although Amn James looked upset, if he wanted to talk about it, he could have said something when Amn Brown asked if he was ok. You aren't even sure you'd say to Amn James if he was still here.

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

    Amn Brown (to Amn James and you): I'm fine. I'm taking my lunch break now.
      Amn James leaves the room.

question: Imagine you decide the best course of action is to say something directly to Amn James. What do you say?
students_answer: Ask him how he's doing. Provide him information on the SARC, restricted and unrestricted reporting, and let him know you're happy to do a warm handoff if interested. Continue to check on him. Also let him know that expedited transfer may be an option if needed.

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer:
Find a way to distract Jacob so that Ken can ask Megan how she feels, whether she's doing ok, and whether she wants Ken to find an excuse to separate her and Jacob. 

Have Ken ask Megan to help him get some waters for their group. Sit down in Megan's spot next to Jacob to get a better read on whether Megan needs some distance.

prompt:
Showing Gratitude

Part 1

You work in a warehouse. While organizing inventory, you overhear a conversation between SrA Johnson (your friend and coworker) and 1stLt Franco (your leader). At first, the conversation seems like normal business talk, but soon the tone changes.

    SrA Johnson: If I fail this review, then I don't get a promotion, and I'll be reassigned OCONUS.
      1stLt Franco: I hear you, Johnson, but sometimes, it's not just about the work. There are... other ways to get ahead around here. You know what I mean?
    SrA Johnson: No, what do you mean?
      1stLt Franco: Listen, I've got influence around here. I can pull some strings to move you up the ranks, but you'd need to... show me some gratitude. This is just how things are done around here.
    SrA Johnson: It sounds like you're suggesting…
      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer:
Approach SrA Johnson separately and tell him what you heard. Ask him how he would like you to support him.

Report the situation to someone higher in the leadership chain or, ideally, someone outside the leadership chain.

      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.
question: Imagine you decide the best course of action is to say something directly to either SrA Johnson or 1stLt Franco. Who do you speak to and what do you say?
students_answer: I would speak to SrA Johnson to let him know what you heard/saw and to reiterate that what he experienced was not normal. If SrA Johnson didn't want to tell anyone what happened, I would raise the situation with the Shirt and possibly some outside agencies. The challenge with going to Lt Franco is that this person cannot be trusted and I would not want to give him time to build excuses or come up with a defense in advance of others talking with him.

prompt:
My Roommate's Arrest

Part 1

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

While you've always considered yourself a good friend and someone who stands up for what's right, you're struggling with what to do in this situation. Jordan, your closest friend, has a habit of joking about sensitive topics without realizing the impact, so he probably didn't mean any harm.

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: If the woman has female friends at the party, they could start dancing with her and get her away from the guy. Anyone could have intervened upon seeing them head towards a room. Frankly someone should have told the guy who wanted to have sex with the 'drunk girl' to back off.

prompt:

Part 1

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

While you've always considered yourself a good friend and someone who stands up for what's right, you're struggling with what to do in this situation. Jordan, your closest friend, has a habit of joking about sensitive topics without realizing the impact, so he probably didn't mean any harm.

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”

prompt:

Part 1

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

While you've always considered yourself a good friend and someone who stands up for what's right, you're struggling with what to do in this situation. Jordan, your closest friend, has a habit of joking about sensitive topics without realizing the impact, so he probably didn't mean any harm.

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: Ask Megan to go to the restroom with you and ask her how she is feeling.  Ask Ken to take Jacob away so you can talk with Megan.

prompt:
Date Night

Part 1

You and your partner are on a double date with another military couple: your co-worker and friend, SPC4 Blackwell, and his new girlfriend, Celeste. The evening is light and fun, and the food is delicious!

When Celeste heads to the restroom, you ask Blackwell about his new relationship.

    You: You finally got Celeste to go on a date with you, huh? Lucky devil. How are things going?
    Blackwell: It's great. Celeste is amazing. Smart. Hot. My only complaint: She can be so uptight. I wish she'd loosen up. Good thing they make pills for that, ha!
You and your partner force a strained laugh, just as Blackwell orders another drink for Celeste, but not for himself.

You excuse yourself to go to the restroom. As Blackwell moves to let you pass by, something drops from his pocket with a clink. It's a pill bottle. You slyly kick the pill bottle down the hall and pick it up. The unmarked bottle looks like it contains a sedative, possibly something that could be used to "loosen someone up”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

The whole situation has taken you by surprise. Your mind races with doubts and disbelief that Blackwell would drug his girlfriend:
    What if I'm overreacting? It's probably just Blackwell's meds. Of course he's only kidding, right? He's always making jokes. Blackwell would never do something like that. No way.
Just then, your partner comes up behind you and says:
    Your partner (to you): It was a pill bottle, wasn't it? Blackwell's awkward comment freaked me out. Who says that?! Celeste is still in the bathroom. What should we do?
You don't have much of a relationship with Celeste and you don't want to overstep, especially since she didn't hear Blackwell say that she needs to loosen up with pills. Plus, you just met Celeste, and she might not listen to you anyway.

You're in a nice restaurant filled with other people and don't want to cause a scene by saying something to him to create unnecessary tension between Blackwell and you at work.

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: Invite Megan to go to the restroom together and ask her how she is feeling about Jacob. Ask Megan if she is ready to go to remove her out of the situation and it gives her the opportunity to get out of the situation.

prompt:
Showing Gratitude

Part 1

You work in a warehouse. While organizing inventory, you overhear a conversation between SrA Johnson (your friend and coworker) and 1stLt Franco (your leader). At first, the conversation seems like normal business talk, but soon the tone changes.

    SrA Johnson: If I fail this review, then I don't get a promotion, and I'll be reassigned OCONUS.
      1stLt Franco: I hear you, Johnson, but sometimes, it's not just about the work. There are... other ways to get ahead around here. You know what I mean?
    SrA Johnson: No, what do you mean?
      1stLt Franco: Listen, I've got influence around here. I can pull some strings to move you up the ranks, but you'd need to... show me some gratitude. This is just how things are done around here.
    SrA Johnson: It sounds like you're suggesting…
      1stLt Franco (cuts him off): Look, you're a smart guy, Johnson. I know what you want, and you know what I want. We can make an arrangement that helps us both get “a head”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You're still processing what you overheard. 1stLt Franco's offer about "showing gratitude" in exchange for a promotion seems coercive, but you're unsure whether to intervene. 1stLt Franco is influential within the team and a higher rank than you and SrA Johnson. You're worried that saying something could cause serious professional consequences.

Even though SrA Johnson is gay, 1stLt Franco is a total “ladies' man”, so it's hard to picture him asking SrA Johnson for sexual favors. Plus, you were eavesdropping. Maybe you didn't hear things correctly?

There's also the issue of SrA Johnson's role in all this. Maybe he doesn't feel pressured or coerced? After all, SrA Johnson didn't speak up or reject 1stLt Franco's offer. Maybe SrA Johnson is cool with it? Saying something to SrA Johnson would be awkward if you're wrong, and you not even sure what you'd say.

Either way, this is definitely none of your business. Still, you can't shake the feeling that 1stLt Franco's behavior is crossing a line.

prompt:
My Roommate's Arrest

Part 1

You're grabbing lunch with a group of friends—Jordan, Alex, and Jamie. The conversation turns to current events after Jordan brings up a headline in the news.

    Jordan: Hey, did you guys hear that my former roommate was arrested last night?! Crazy, right?
      You: No way! What happened?
    Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.
    Alex: That's so messed up. You can't just change your mind like that. Sounds like another female who regrets her bad decisions.
    Jamie: Yeah, what's up with girls getting trashed, hanging all over guys, and then crying rape? I mean, if she didn't want it, why did she let him take her to a room?

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

While you've always considered yourself a good friend and someone who stands up for what's right, you're struggling with what to do in this situation. Jordan, your closest friend, has a habit of joking about sensitive topics without realizing the impact, so he probably didn't mean any harm.

You don't want to make things awkward, especially because everyone is laughing and doesn't seem bothered. Another friend's sister was sexually assaulted and died by suicide last year, so you know this is a serious issue.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: do nothing or confront him later if it's bothering you. it can be hard in the moment.

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer:
1. Megan is an adult. Let it be. Because if she was actually into him and was just wasted, then if you confront the guy, she might ask you why you did that and you would be really embarassed. 
2. Confront Jacob.

prompt:
Date Night

Part 1

You and your partner are on a double date with another military couple: your co-worker and friend, SPC4 Blackwell, and his new girlfriend, Celeste. The evening is light and fun, and the food is delicious!

When Celeste heads to the restroom, you ask Blackwell about his new relationship.

    You: You finally got Celeste to go on a date with you, huh? Lucky devil. How are things going?
    Blackwell: It's great. Celeste is amazing. Smart. Hot. My only complaint: She can be so uptight. I wish she'd loosen up. Good thing they make pills for that, ha!
You and your partner force a strained laugh, just as Blackwell orders another drink for Celeste, but not for himself.

You excuse yourself to go to the restroom. As Blackwell moves to let you pass by, something drops from his pocket with a clink. It's a pill bottle. You slyly kick the pill bottle down the hall and pick it up. The unmarked bottle looks like it contains a sedative, possibly something that could be used to "loosen someone up”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

The whole situation has taken you by surprise. Your mind races with doubts and disbelief that Blackwell would drug his girlfriend:
    What if I'm overreacting? It's probably just Blackwell's meds. Of course he's only kidding, right? He's always making jokes. Blackwell would never do something like that. No way.
Just then, your partner comes up behind you and says:
    Your partner (to you): It was a pill bottle, wasn't it? Blackwell's awkward comment freaked me out. Who says that?! Celeste is still in the bathroom. What should we do?
You don't have much of a relationship with Celeste and you don't want to overstep, especially since she didn't hear Blackwell say that she needs to loosen up with pills. Plus, you just met Celeste, and she might not listen to you anyway.

You're in a nice restaurant filled with other people and don't want to cause a scene by saying something to him to create unnecessary tension between Blackwell and you at work.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: Get Celeste and assist her home with a fellow female. Tell Blackwell that  his behavior is not becoming of an Airman and he better re-think his words and actions.

prompt:
Date Night

Part 1

You and your partner are on a double date with another military couple: your co-worker and friend, SPC4 Blackwell, and his new girlfriend, Celeste. The evening is light and fun, and the food is delicious!

When Celeste heads to the restroom, you ask Blackwell about his new relationship.

    You: You finally got Celeste to go on a date with you, huh? Lucky devil. How are things going?
    Blackwell: It's great. Celeste is amazing. Smart. Hot. My only complaint: She can be so uptight. I wish she'd loosen up. Good thing they make pills for that, ha!
You and your partner force a strained laugh, just as Blackwell orders another drink for Celeste, but not for himself.

You excuse yourself to go to the restroom. As Blackwell moves to let you pass by, something drops from his pocket with a clink. It's a pill bottle. You slyly kick the pill bottle down the hall and pick it up. The unmarked bottle looks like it contains a sedative, possibly something that could be used to "loosen someone up”.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

The whole situation has taken you by surprise. Your mind races with doubts and disbelief that Blackwell would drug his girlfriend:
    What if I'm overreacting? It's probably just Blackwell's meds. Of course he's only kidding, right? He's always making jokes. Blackwell would never do something like that. No way.
Just then, your partner comes up behind you and says:
    Your partner (to you): It was a pill bottle, wasn't it? Blackwell's awkward comment freaked me out. Who says that?! Celeste is still in the bathroom. What should we do?
You don't have much of a relationship with Celeste and you don't want to overstep, especially since she didn't hear Blackwell say that she needs to loosen up with pills. Plus, you just met Celeste, and she might not listen to you anyway.

You're in a nice restaurant filled with other people and don't want to cause a scene by saying something to him to create unnecessary tension between Blackwell and you at work.
question: Imagine you decide the best course of action is to say something directly to SPC4 Blackwell. What do you say?
students_answer: Dude, Your behavior is not becoming of an Airman. You better rethink your actions and words immediately. In the meantime, we're taking Celeste home.

students_answer: lives and reputations are saved!
categories:
| Building Bystander Skills & Confidence  | Taking action because it  builds skills, confidence, or readiness to act in future situations. | Doing something now makes it easier to act the next time., Taking action strengthens your ability to recognize when and how to step in. |
| Bystander Avoidance / Diffusion of Responsibility | Avoids personal responsibility for acting, assuming someone else will intervene or that it's not one's place to respond. | “Someone else will step in if it's really a problem.”, “It's not my place to get involved—it's between them.”, “No one else seems concerned, so I don't need to say anything.” |
| Changing Perpetrator Behavior & Attitudes | Taking action because it influences the person causing harm to reflect, reconsider, or change their behavior and attitudes. | The individual may come to understand that their behavior is inappropriate or unacceptable, The bystander might be able to stop the perpetrator from acting on harmful intentions. |
| Cultural or Peer-Norm Justification | Uses social norms, workplace culture, or peer expectations to justify inaction or normalize harmful behavior | “That's just part of the culture here; everyone jokes like that.”, “It's better not to rock the boat—people will think you're uptight.”, “If I speak up, it'll just make things awkward in the group.” |

| Perpetrator-Excusing | Defends or rationalizes the behavior of the person causing harm, often framing it as unintentional or acceptable under the circumstances | “Calling them out might embarrass them—they probably didn't mean any harm.”, “You shouldn't interfere; that's just how some people talk.”, “They didn't intend to offend anyone, so there's no problem.” |
| Preventing Future Harms | Taking action because it helps stop a situation from getting worse or prevents more severe forms of harm (e.g., harassment escalating to assault). | Speaking out against this behavior helps now prevent more severe forms of sexual assault or harassment in the future., It might prevent a sexual assault from occuring. |
| Protecting Teams | Taking action because it strengthens trust, cohesion, and mutual respect within a group, team, or community. | Intervention helps maintain trust and respect within the group, unit, squad or battalion., Taking action reinforces mutual trust and respect among members of the group or unit. |
| Recognition and Rewards for the Bystander | Taking action because it results in acknowledgemnt, appreciation or other tangible rewards for the bystander. | Doing the right thing can lead to recognition, commendation, or confidence in your character., Those who act to protect others often become examples for future training and mentorship. |
| Shaping Norms & Culture Change | Taking action because it challenges harmful norms and helps establish respect, safety, and accountability as community standards. | It stops sexual violence from being seen as normal, acceptable, or “not a big deal.”, Addressing the behavior reinforces that safety and dignity are shared values. |
| Supporting the Victim | Taking action because the victim feels supported, validated, less isolated, or safer in the moment. | Taking action will help the victim feel validated and supported, To save the victim from being harmed by the perpetrator. |
| Victim-blaming | Attributes responsibility for the harm to the person experiencing it rather than the person causing it. Implies the victim provoked, consented to, or could have prevented the behavior. | “If it really bothered them, they would've said something.”, “If someone flirts a lot, they shouldn't be surprised when others cross the line.” , “They shouldn't have gone to that party if they didn't want that kind of attention.” |

prompt:
Group Work Chat

Part 1

You are working with SPC2 Jones, SPC2 O'Leary, SPC2 Redman, and CMSgt Lovelace, everyone's superior. As the superior, CMSgt Lovelace set up a group text message for work. It's the end of year, and the team is discussing wrapping up a project when the conversation shifts.

    SPC2 Jones: Check this out. Santa's coming to wrap up our project!! LOL
      SPC2 Jones sends a text with a meme of a shirtless Santa holding presents and flexing his biceps and tags CMSgt Lovelace. CMSgt Lovelace “likes” the meme.
    SPC2 O'Leary: I've got something that Santa can wrap up… LOL
      CMSgt Lovelace “laughs” at SPC2 O'Leary's message.
    CMSgt Lovelace: C'mon, @Jones, you know that's not appropriate since we're an inclusive workplace ;) Amirite @Redman?
      CMSgt Lovelace sends a nude picture of a black Santa and tags SPC2 Redman, who is black.
    SPC2 Redman: Real funny. I don't want to see that. Maybe we shouldn't be sharing stuff like that on a work chat, Ma'am?
    CMSgt Lovelace: Oh, come on, it's just a joke. Don't be so uptight, @Redman.
    SPC2 Jones: Chill out. It's the end of the year, and we're just blowing off steam.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You've been working with this group for a while and get along with everyone, so you're not sure what to do. You don't want to jeopardize your job or friendships, especially because it's only SPC2 Redman who seems offended. Plus, SPC2 O'Leary, who is gay, wasn't bothered by the images. And while SPC2 Jones started it, you know she probably regrets that the situation got out of hand.

CMSgt Lovelace is usually crass and SPC2 O'Leary has told you before that he's put off by her homophobic jokes. You sense things could resolve on their own because SPC2 Redman mentioned reporting CMSgt Lovelace for her offensive and racist comments.

prompt:
A Private Conversation

Part 1

You are stationed at a military base working with SSgt Quinn, Amn James, and Amn Brown. You notice SSgt Quinn and Amn James step into the hallway for a private conversation. At first, it seems like normal work talk, until you overhear SSgt Quinn's comment.

    SSgt Quinn (to Amn James): I am a sexual being. Some things cannot be controlled.
      Amn James laughs nervously at SSgt Quinn.
The exchange sounds off, but you're on a deadline, so you ask Amn Brown to check on things. Amn Brown steps out into the hall when you hear a struggle, presumably between SSgt Quinn and Amn James. Amn Brown returns to say:
    Amn Brown (to you): I don't know what exactly happened, but SSgt Quinn pinned Amn James against the wall and grabbed his crotch. He pushed her away, and SSgt Quinn walked off like it was nothing!

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

Just then, Amn James returns to the workspace, visibly shaken.
    Amn James (to Adm Brown): You ok, man?
    Amn Brown (to Amn James and you): I'm fine. I'm taking my lunch break now.
      Amn James leaves the room.

prompt:
A Private Conversation

Part 1

You are stationed at a military base working with SSgt Quinn, Amn James, and Amn Brown. You notice SSgt Quinn and Amn James step into the hallway for a private conversation. At first, it seems like normal work talk, until you overhear SSgt Quinn's comment.

    SSgt Quinn (to Amn James): I am a sexual being. Some things cannot be controlled.
      Amn James laughs nervously at SSgt Quinn.
The exchange sounds off, but you're on a deadline, so you ask Amn Brown to check on things. Amn Brown steps out into the hall when you hear a struggle, presumably between SSgt Quinn and Amn James. Amn Brown returns to say:
    Amn Brown (to you): I don't know what exactly happened, but SSgt Quinn pinned Amn James against the wall and grabbed his crotch. He pushed her away, and SSgt Quinn walked off like it was nothing!

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

Just then, Amn James returns to the workspace, visibly shaken.
    Amn James (to Adm Brown): You ok, man?
    Amn Brown (to Amn James and you): I'm fine. I'm taking my lunch break now.
      Amn James leaves the room.
    Amn Brown (to you): Dude, no one's going to believe a guy got assaulted by a woman.
You didn't directly witness what happened and you're not sure what to do. You want to help, but the situation seems resolved after both SSgt Quinn and Amn Brown left the workplace.

Although Amn James looked upset, if he wanted to talk about it, he could have said something when Amn Brown asked if he was ok. You aren't even sure you'd say to Amn James if he was still here.
question: Imagine you decide the best course of action is to say something directly to Amn James. What do you say?
students_answer: I noticed you seemed uncomfortable and I heard her say something inappropriate to you before I heard a scuffle in the hallway. Are you okay? Would you like to talk about what happened? I'm here for you.

prompt:
Dancing at the Club

Part 1

It's Friday night and you're getting drinks with your friends Ken and Megan after work. Jacob, a buddy from high school, walks in and you invite him to hang out with the group.

Everyone is chatting and getting along. You all decide to go to a second location—a nearby club. The music is loud, everyone's drinking, and the vibe is fun.

Megan and Jacob are hitting it off, and you sense chemistry between them. As the night goes on, Jacob becomes increasingly close to Megan, first by putting his arms around Megan's shoulders, then touching her thighs repeatedly. Megan keeps looking at you and Ken, almost as if she's seeking reassurance, but she never pulls away from Jacob.

Ken notices Megan's body language and leans over to you.

    Ken: Hey, do you think Megan's okay with Jacob
      You: I'm not sure. They look fine to me.
    Ken: It looks weird to me. Jacob's being really handsy, and Megan doesn't seem into it.

Part 2 - Scroll to continue reading

You think that Ken is reading too much into the situation and interpret the situation differently. Jacob is an old fiend, and he doesn't mean any harm. He's just drinking and having a good time with a pretty girl.

Plus, you believe Megan would speak up for herself if someone crossed a line. On the other hand, Megan has been drinking, so maybe you should step in? You worry that if you're wrong, then Megan will think you don't trust her judgment or that she needs saving because she's a girl.

It feels like you're walking a fine line. A part of you wonders if you should just let Ken handle it since he's the one who is so concerned.
question: Active bystanders decide how to act in a risky situation based on what they know about a situation and the people involved. After reading part 2 of the scenario, identify two acceptable ways to respond in this situation.
students_answer: Get Megan away from Jacob and ask her in private if she is okay with the touching. Another way could be to mouth or sign to Megan asking if she is okay when Jacob is not looking.

| Document | Create a formal or informal record or report through proper channels. | Make a mental note of what you observed—who was there, what time, and what Jacob did., If you suspect something serious (e.g., drugging, assault), write down the sequence of events afterward while it's fresh., If Megan chooses to report, share your account privately and confidentially with investigators or advocates. |
| Hostile Confrontation | Confront in a hostile, threatening, shaming, or emotionally charged way that increases interpersonal conflict.     Jordan: Apparently, my old roommate was at a party. This drunk chick starts dancing all close and coming on strong, you know? Well, they went back to his room and had sex. Three days later, she went to the police saying it was sexual assault.

You want to say something, but Alex is known for being defensive when challenged especially in a group setting, and Jamie has always been a jerk, often dismisses situations as "overblown" or as “just a joke.”
